text:
### Abstract

Can international development ever truly improve the lives of the world’s poorest citizens? Rochelle A. Burgess’s *Rethinking Global Health: Frameworks of Power* challenges us to consider a deeply unsettling possibility—that international global health, a system that is supposed to be saving lives worldwide, remains stubbornly enmeshed in the very same systems of power that determined whether people would live or die before global health began trying to fix these problems. Through a critical analysis of everyday practices, global health governance, and global inequality, Burgess shines a light on the uncomfortable truths of global health that policymakers, non-governmental organizations, and academics would rather not discuss, including the fact that the international efforts to promote universal health coverages and global control of infectious diseases almost always neglect the complex social and political context in which the lives of the world’s poorest are lived.

Power, Change, and Community Health draws on a range of perspectives from the field of community health psychology as well as sociology and decolonial scholarship to illuminate the many ways that power operates in the structural, disciplinary, hegemonic and interpersonal spheres. Through rich and vivid case studies of the dynamics of power and its effects, Ruth Burgess explores four pivotal sites of struggle around the world: • legislation around women’s rights in Uganda and the gendered nature of the politics of gender at the national level in post-colony • the phenomenon of ‘psychiatrization’ of distress across South Africa and Colombia • the increasing securitization of the Ebola outbreak in West Africa • structural failure and ‘incommensurability’ between different forms of knowledge and different social and cultural realities in relation to cholera in post-earthquake Haiti Burgess demonstrates how power operates in top-down models of intervention and that medicalizing social conditions and the dismissal of local actors and their knowledges can limit the potential of initiatives and hinder transformation.

Global health is argued to be a practice based on a transformable theory of change. An adaptation of the work of social scientist Patricia Hill Collins entitled the “matrix of domination” is used by Burgess to describe how policies affect inequality in multiple ways that lead to injustice, inequity and harm to communities. An alternative praxis for global health named “Transformative Global Health” is proposed; one that is radical in the sense that it transforms, rather than just ameliorates, the structure of inequality and prioritizes community needs while challenging institutional global health actors to give up power. This volume is an invaluable learning tool for students, faculty and global health practitioners all eager to move the practice of global health forward towards greater health equity and social justice.
